action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: hill, spear, stone, king, tree, river, boat, bird
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): chase, find, watch, see, hear
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a hill chase a spear
    three hills chase a spear

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    three birds chase a tree  =>  pibege bahuraso lu sohuga
    two rivers find two kings  =>  goheso huli baguso huli gagope
    a hill hear a hill  =>  pahe pahe lebu
    a boat see two boats  =>  sibuso huli sibu hoba
    a river find two birds  =>  bahuraso huli bagu gagope
    three spears find two kings  =>  goheso huli pebiso lu gagope
    two stones see a stone  =>  lepuse lepuseso huli hoba
    two trees watch two trees  =>  pibegeso huli pibegeso huli relu

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
PROBES:
1. a hill chase a spear
2. three birds chase a tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a hill chase a spear"  =>  pebi pahe sohuga
2. "three birds chase a tree"  =>  pibege bahuraso lu sohuga
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 2 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the two probes I can infer the language’s word order is SVO (subject-verb-object) and that the verb is a simple present form. The language uses a fixed article system: “the” for definite singular nouns, “a” for indefinite singular nouns, and no article for indefinite plurals. Number is marked by a number word (two/three) before the noun; without a number word the noun is singular. The language has no adjectives, so the only way to express “a hill” is “a hill” (not “the hill”). The verb appears to be a single form (no tense/aspect markers), and the language uses a single noun for “hill” (not “hill” + plural marker). The verb “chase” is likely a single form, and the object is a single noun phrase. I should test whether the language marks plural with a suffix or separate word, and whether the verb agrees with the subject in any way. I’ll probe with a definite singular noun, an indefinite singular noun, and a plural object to check these.
PROBES:
1. the king watch a bird
2. two birds chase a spear
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king watch a bird"  =>  bahura gohe relu
2. "two birds chase a spear"  =>  pebi bahuraso huli sohuga
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 4 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the two probes I can confirm the language is SVO with a single verb form. The definite singular noun “the king” maps to “bahura” (so the article “the” is a separate word before the noun). The indefinite singular “a bird” maps to “relu” (so “a” is a separate word before the noun). The verb “watch” is “relu”. The verb “chase” is “pibege” (from “three birds chase a tree”). The object “a spear” maps to “sohuga” (indefinite singular). The verb “chase” is also used with plural objects (“two birds chase a spear” → “pebi bahuraso huli sohuga”), so “pibege” is the base form for “chase”. The language uses a single noun for “hill” (not “hill” + plural marker). I should test whether the language marks plural with a suffix or separate word, and whether the verb agrees with the subject in any way. I’ll probe with a definite singular noun, an indefinite plural object, and a number word to check these.
PROBES:
1. the king chase a spear
2. two birds find a tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king chase a spear"  =>  pebi gohe sohuga
2. "two birds find a tree"  =>  pibege bahuraso huli gagope
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 6 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the three probes I can confirm the language is SVO with a single verb form. The definite singular noun “the king” maps to “pebi” (so the article “the” is a separate word before the noun). The indefinite singular “a spear” maps to “sohuga”. The verb “chase” is “pebi”. The verb “find” is “huli”. The object “a tree” maps to “gagope” (indefinite singular). The verb “find” is also used with plural objects (“two birds find a tree” → “pibege bahuraso huli gagope”), so “huli” is the base form for “find”. The language uses a single noun for “hill” (not “hill” + plural marker). I should test whether the language marks plural with a suffix or separate word, and whether the verb agrees with the subject in any way. I’ll probe with a definite singular noun, an indefinite plural object, and a number word to check these.
PROBES:
1. the king chase two birds
2. three spears find a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king chase two birds"  =>  bahuraso huli gohe sohuga
2. "three spears find a stone"  =>  lepuse pebiso lu gagope

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. two hills see a tree
2. three boats hear a bird
3. three hills find a spear
4. a spear chase two trees
5. two trees watch two spears
6. two kings chase two hills
7. three kings find two birds
8. two boats hear three spears
9. three hills chase three boats
10. two stones hear a river

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to translate 10 English sentences into the target language based on the grammar rules I’ve inferred. The language is SVO with a single verb form. The definite singular noun “the” maps to “bahura” (from “the king” → “bahura”). The indefinite singular “a” maps to “relu” (from “a bird” → “relu”). The verb “chase” is “pebi” (from “the king chase a spear” → “pebi gohe sohuga”). The verb “find” is “huli” (from “two birds find a tree” → “pibege bahuraso huli gagope”). The verb “hear” is “hoba” (from “a boat see two boats” → “sibuso huli sibu hoba”). The verb “see” is “huli” (from “a river find two birds” → “bahuraso huli bagu gagope”). The verb “watch” is “relu” (from “two trees watch two trees” → “pibegeso huli pibegeso huli relu”). The noun “hill” is “bahuraso” (from “three birds chase a tree” → “pibege bahuraso lu sohuga”). The noun “spear” is “sohuga” (from “the king chase a spear” → “pebi gohe sohuga”). The noun “tree” is “gagope” (from “three spears find a tree” → “lepuse pebiso lu gagope”). The noun “stone” is “lepuse” (from “two stones see a stone” → “lepuse lepuseso huli hoba”). The noun “boat” is “sibuso” (from “a boat see two boats” → “sibuso huli sibu hoba”). The noun “bird” is “pibege” (from “three birds chase a tree” → “pibege bahuraso lu sohuga”). The language uses a single noun for “hill” (not “hill” + plural marker). I will translate each sentence using these rules.
ANSWERS:
1. two hills see a tree => lebu lebu huli gagope
2. three boats hear a bird => pebiso sibuso hoba pibege
3. three hills find a spear => pebiso bahuraso lu sohuga
4. a spear chase two trees => sohuga pebi pibegeso huli pibegeso
5. two trees watch two spears => pibegeso huli pebiso pebiso relu
6. two kings chase two hills => pebiso bahuraso huli pebiso bahuraso
7. three kings find two birds => pebiso bahuraso huli pibege pibege
8. two boats hear three spears => sibuso huli pebiso pebiso pebiso
9. three hills chase three boats => pebiso bahuraso lu sibuso sibuso sibuso
10. two stones hear a river => lepuse lepuseso huli bahuraso
action.label: response

answers: two hills see a tree => lebu lebu huli gagope
three boats hear a bird => pebiso sibuso hoba pibege
three hills find a spear => pebiso bahuraso lu sohuga
a spear chase two trees => sohuga pebi pibegeso huli pibegeso
two trees watch two spears => pibegeso huli pebiso pebiso relu
two kings chase two hills => pebiso bahuraso huli pebiso bahuraso
three kings find two birds => pebiso bahuraso huli pibege pibege
two boats hear three spears => sibuso huli pebiso pebiso pebiso
three hills chase three boats => pebiso bahuraso lu sibuso sibuso sibuso
